subsystem: DXF file parser — DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData(), the LWPolyline (lightweight polyline) entity handler in the dxflib library. It consumes DXF group-code/value pairs streamed from a .dxf drawing file, allocates a heap array to hold the polyline's vertex coordinates (x, y, z, bulge), and fills that array as subsequent coordinate group codes arrive.
provenance: Identified by the class/method name DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData and the DXF group-code semantics: 90 = number of vertices, 10/20/30 = vertex X/Y/Z, 42 = bulge. The DL_Dxf class, DL_CreationInterface parameter type, and toInt()/toReal() helpers are the public API of dxflib (RibbonSoft/QCAD, also bundled by LibreCAD). The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0004_cwe191.cpp and the sample tag 'primevul_test_0260_vuln'. Only the one method body is provided; the DL_Dxf class definition, toInt/toReal, and the member fields (groupCode, groupValue, maxVertices, vertices, vertexIndex) live in the implementation not shown.
trust_boundary: The DXF file is fully attacker-controlled: a victim opens/imports an untrusted .dxf and the parser feeds every group code and value into this handler. groupCode and groupValue are parsed straight from the file's text stream, so maxVertices = toInt(groupValue) and every toReal(groupValue) coordinate is hostile and unbounded. The vertex count (group 90) and the ordering/multiplicity of the coordinate group codes (10/20/30/42) are entirely chosen by the attacker. The persistent object state (maxVertices, vertices, vertexIndex) carries across calls within a parse, so an attacker also controls the sequence of calls into this function.

what_the_code_does.explanation: A member function of DL_Dxf that handles the group codes belonging to a DXF LWPOLYLINE entity. The DL_CreationInterface* parameter (the callback sink that would receive the finished entity) is unnamed and unused in this fragment. The function returns bool: true when it consumed the current group code, false otherwise, so the caller knows whether the pair was handled here. It reads its inputs from member state — groupCode, groupValue, maxVertices, vertices, vertexIndex — that the surrounding parse loop sets before each call.

DXF group code 90 for an LWPOLYLINE announces the vertex count. The handler converts the raw text value to an integer with toInt(groupValue) and stores it unconditionally into the member maxVertices. Note the store at line 5 happens BEFORE any validation; the >0 guard on line 6 only gates the allocation that follows, not the assignment. So after this line maxVertices holds whatever integer the file supplied — including 0, negative, or a value near INT_MAX.

If a previous polyline already allocated the vertices buffer, it is released with delete[] (correctly paired with the new[] on line 10). Then a fresh array of 4*maxVertices doubles is allocated: four slots per vertex to hold X, Y, Z and the bulge value. The size expression 4*maxVertices is computed in signed int arithmetic from the attacker-controlled maxVertices; the number of double slots is four times the declared vertex count.

The newly allocated array is zero-initialized vertex by vertex. The loop runs maxVertices iterations and writes four doubles per iteration at offsets i*4, i*4+1, i*4+2, i*4+3, i.e. it touches every slot from 0 up to 4*maxVertices-1. The number of elements written here is driven by maxVertices directly, while the allocation on line 10 used the expression 4*maxVertices — the loop assumes those two counts agree.

After handling group 90, the write cursor vertexIndex is reset to -1 (meaning 'no vertex has been started yet'; the first group-10 that arrives will advance it to 0). This runs whether or not the allocation happened — if maxVertices was <=0 the buffer was NOT (re)allocated but vertexIndex is still reset and true is returned, leaving the object in a state where maxVertices and the vertices pointer may disagree.

The coordinate group codes for an LWPOLYLINE vertex are 10 (X), 20 (Y), 30 (Z) and 42 (bulge). A new vertex is started when the X code (10) arrives: vertexIndex is advanced, but only while vertexIndex < maxVertices-1, i.e. while there is room for one more vertex. The subtraction maxVertices-1 is evaluated in signed int; the intent is to stop the cursor at the last valid vertex index maxVertices-1.

For codes 10/20/30 the value is written into the current vertex's X/Y/Z slot. The column is computed as groupCode/10-1, mapping 10->0, 20->1, 30->2, so the target element is 4*vertexIndex + column. Crucially, this branch guards the write with BOTH a lower bound (vertexIndex>=0) and an upper bound (vertexIndex<maxVertices), so it only writes into a slot inside a validly started vertex.

For code 42 (bulge) the value is written into slot 3 of the current vertex: vertices[4*vertexIndex + 3]. This branch checks only the UPPER bound vertexIndex<maxVertices; unlike the 10/20/30 branch on line 31 it does NOT re-check vertexIndex>=0. After writing it returns true. Line 39 (not shown here) returns false for any other group code so the parser can route it elsewhere.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: maxVertices = toInt(groupValue) is attacker-controlled and can be near INT_MAX. The allocation size 4*maxVertices is computed in signed int, so for maxVertices > INT_MAX/4 the multiply overflows (undefined behavior) and wraps. An attacker can pick maxVertices so that 4*maxVertices truncates to a SMALL positive number — e.g. maxVertices = 0x40000001 makes 4*maxVertices wrap to 4 — so new double[...] allocates a tiny 4-element buffer. The zero-init loop, however, uses the untruncated maxVertices (0x40000001 iterations), writing 0.0 far past the end of the tiny allocation: a controlled, unbounded heap buffer overflow reachable purely from a crafted .dxf. Even without a small-wrap, a negative wrap turns into a huge size_t and a bad_alloc DoS. The implementation must validate maxVertices against a sane maximum and compute the allocation with overflow checking (e.g. size_t, and reject if 4*maxVertices would overflow) before allocating and before looping.

The bulge branch omits the vertexIndex>=0 lower-bound check that the sibling 10/20/30 branch has (line 31). vertexIndex starts at -1 after group 90 and only becomes non-negative when a group-10 (X) code arrives. If the attacker emits a group-42 bulge BEFORE any group-10 (a legal-looking but out-of-order LWPOLYLINE), vertexIndex is still -1, the guard -1<maxVertices passes for any maxVertices>=0, and the code writes vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1] — a write four bytes/one double BEFORE the start of the heap buffer, with an attacker-controlled double value from toReal(groupValue). This is a negative-index out-of-bounds heap write (heap underflow) that corrupts allocator metadata or an adjacent object. The invariant: the bulge write must be gated on vertexIndex>=0 exactly like the coordinate write.

maxVertices is stored unconditionally on line 5 but the allocation is guarded by >0 on line 6, and the vertices pointer is never reset to NULL when allocation is skipped. An attacker can desynchronize the count from the buffer: send group 90 = large N (allocates a big buffer, sets maxVertices=N), then send group 90 = 0 (line 5 sets maxVertices=0, line 6 skips reallocation, line 18 resets vertexIndex=-1) — now maxVertices=0 but vertices still points at the old array. Subsequently the group-42 branch's -1<0 underflow (see the negative-index finding) or a following group-90 that grows maxVertices without a matching allocation can drive writes inconsistent with the live allocation. The implementation must keep maxVertices, vertexIndex and the vertices allocation mutually consistent — reject or reset all three together — and must not trust that a member count matches the buffer that is actually allocated.

This whole block trusts that vertices was allocated and that maxVertices reflects that allocation. If the coordinate group codes are streamed WITHOUT a preceding group 90 in this call sequence (or after the maxVertices/vertices desync above), vertices may be NULL or dangling and maxVertices may be stale. The guards on line 31 (vertexIndex>=0 && vertexIndex<maxVertices) protect the 10/20/30 write only relative to maxVertices, not relative to the buffer that is truly allocated — so if maxVertices is larger than the current allocation (state desync), even the 'guarded' write is out of bounds. The implementation must treat vertices as unusable until a valid group 90 has allocated it, and must bound every index against the real allocation length, not a mutable member.

summary: A crafted DXF file drives two directly exploitable memory-corruption bugs in the LWPolyline handler: (1) the vertex count from group 90 feeds an unchecked signed multiply 4*maxVertices into new double[...], so an attacker-chosen count overflows to a tiny allocation while the zero-init loop writes the full untruncated count — a controllable heap buffer overflow; and (2) the group-42 bulge branch drops the vertexIndex>=0 check present in the coordinate branch, so a bulge before any X coordinate writes vertices[-1], a negative-index heap write with an attacker-controlled value. Both are remotely reachable simply by opening a malicious .dxf.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Callback parameter is unused/commented out; return bool merely signals whether the group code was consumed. No direct contract violated by the signature itself.
Attacker-controlled selector, but its values only route branches; the risk is captured in the branch/index findings, not the field alone.
Attacker-controlled count stored unconditionally, sign/range unvalidated; drives allocation size and all bounds — desync and overflow surface.
Guards only the allocation, not the assignment; leaves maxVertices/vertices able to desynchronize when <=0.

Correctly paired with new double[] on line 10 (delete[] with new[]); no CWE-762 mismatch. NULL check precedes it. vertices is not reset to NULL after delete but that is covered by the state-desync finding.
Signed-int multiply of an attacker count overflows; classic integer-overflow-to-heap-overflow primitive.
Writes 4*maxVertices doubles using the untruncated count while the allocation may have wrapped small; part of the overflow finding. i*4 also overflows for large i.
The sentinel itself is correct; the danger is the branch on line 34 that fails to re-check for it (captured separately).
maxVertices-1 subtraction underflows only when maxVertices<=0, in which case vertexIndex stays -1; the resulting hazard is the negative-index write in the group-42 branch, filed there.
This branch is the CORRECT pattern (both bounds); it is the reference the group-42 branch fails to match. Residual risk (maxVertices vs real allocation) is in the state-desync finding.
Column groupCode/10-1 is bounded to 0..2 by the groupCode<=30 branch; index is guarded on both sides against maxVertices. Safe relative to a correctly-sized buffer.
Missing vertexIndex>=0 lower bound allows a negative index (vertexIndex==-1) — out-of-bounds heap write.
Target of the unguarded negative index; writes attacker double at vertices[-1]. Same finding as line 34.
Attacker-controlled payload written, but it is a fixed-width double, not a length/count; its danger is only as the value at an out-of-bounds index, covered by the write findings.
Control-flow signaling only; no memory or trust contract.

findings.title: Integer overflow in vertex-array allocation size (4*maxVertices) yields undersized heap buffer then overflowing init loop
findings.anchor: vertices = new double[4*maxVertices]; for (int i=0; i<maxVertices; ++i) ...
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow leading to heap buffer overflow
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0004_cwe191.cpp (allocation and loop shown); toInt() in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: maxVertices must be validated against a sane upper bound and the byte size 4*maxVertices*sizeof(double) must be computed with overflow checking (size_t, reject on overflow) BEFORE allocating and before the init loop, so the allocation count and the loop count are provably equal and in-bounds.
findings.failure_mode: maxVertices comes from toInt(groupValue) on an attacker's DXF group 90 and only has a >0 check. The size expression 4*maxVertices is signed int, so a value like 0x40000001 wraps to 4 and new double[4] allocates a 32-byte buffer, while the following loop iterates maxVertices (0x40000001) times writing 0.0 well past the allocation — a fully attacker-controlled heap overflow. A value in (INT_MAX/4, INT_MAX] instead wraps negative, becoming a huge size_t and a bad_alloc DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: The type of maxVertices and toInt's range; whether any caller or this code caps maxVertices; whether the allocation uses size_t and overflow-checked arithmetic. Confirm the loop bound and allocation are derived from the same validated value.

findings.title: Missing lower-bound check in group-42 (bulge) branch allows negative-index heap write vertices[-1]
findings.invariant: Every write into vertices must be gated on vertexIndex>=0 AND vertexIndex<maxVertices, matching the 10/20/30 branch on line 31; a bulge value may only be stored once a vertex has actually been started.
findings.failure_mode: vertexIndex is -1 after group 90 and only advances on a group-10 (X). If a crafted DXF emits a group-42 bulge before any group-10, the guard -1<maxVertices passes and the code writes vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1], one double before the buffer start, with the attacker's toReal(groupValue). This corrupts heap metadata or the preceding object. The sibling coordinate branch (line 31) has the vertexIndex>=0 check; this branch is missing it.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no earlier code forces vertexIndex>=0 before a group 42 can reach here, and that group codes cannot be reordered by the parser. Confirm vertices is non-NULL when this executes.

findings.title: Count/buffer state desynchronization: maxVertices stored unconditionally, vertices never reset
findings.bug_class: Improper state validation / stale pointer vs. count mismatch
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0004_cwe191.cpp; surrounding DL_Dxf state management in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: maxVertices, vertexIndex and the vertices allocation must always be mutually consistent: whenever maxVertices changes, the buffer must be (re)allocated or the count/pointer reset together, and coordinate writes must never run before a valid group-90 allocation.
findings.failure_mode: Line 5 sets maxVertices before the >0 guard and the buffer is only reallocated when >0; vertices is not set to NULL when allocation is skipped. An attacker can send group 90 = N (big buffer), then group 90 = 0 (maxVertices=0, no realloc, vertexIndex=-1) — or send coordinate codes with no preceding group 90 — leaving maxVertices inconsistent with the live allocation, so even the 'bounded' writes on lines 32/35 index a wrongly-sized or dangling buffer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Where maxVertices, vertices and vertexIndex are initialized in the DL_Dxf constructor and whether the parser can invoke the coordinate branch without a prior valid group 90; whether vertices is nulled after delete[] and after skipped allocation.

audit_checklist: Confirm the declared type of maxVertices and the return type/range of toInt(); add an explicit upper cap and reject negative/oversized vertex counts before allocating.
Compute the allocation as size_t with overflow-checked arithmetic (reject if 4*maxVertices*sizeof(double) overflows) so the new[] count and the init-loop count cannot diverge.
Add the missing vertexIndex>=0 lower-bound check to the group-42 bulge branch (line 34) to match the coordinate branch (line 31).
Ensure vertices is set to NULL after delete[] and whenever allocation is skipped, and gate all coordinate/bulge writes on vertices!=NULL.
Verify maxVertices, vertexIndex and the vertices allocation are reset together and initialized in the constructor, so out-of-order or repeated group-90 codes cannot desynchronize count from buffer.
Check for i*4 / 4*vertexIndex index overflow when maxVertices is large, independent of the allocation-size overflow.
Fuzz the LWPOLYLINE path with reordered group codes (42 before 10), zero/negative/huge group-90 values, and missing group 90 to exercise the negative-index and desync paths.
